title: Peru Mining Output Hits Record High Despite Political Uncertainty, Copper at Centre
description: Peru Mining Output Hits Record High Despite Political Uncertainty, Copper at Centre S&P Global Commodity Insights
htmlContent:
Peru Mining Output Hits Record High Despite Political Uncertainty, Copper at Centre

S&P Global Commodity Insights • June 7, 2026

LIMA / LONDON – Peru posted a record monthly mining output in May 2026, with copper production jumping 9.4% year-on-year to approximately 250,000 tonnes, offering a partial counterweight to the supply disruptions that have gripped the copper market in recent months.

The increase was led by strong performances at three of the country's largest copper operations. Anglo American's Quellaveco mine, which ramped to full design capacity in late 2025, contributed a record monthly output of 33,500 tonnes. The MMG-operated Las Bambas mine, which had been plagued by community roadblocks through much of 2024 and early 2025, reported its best month since 2022 after a community agreement brokered by the Peruvian government late last year took hold. Antamina, the BHP and Glencore joint venture, also reported a strong month driven by favourable ore grades.

The record output came despite ongoing political instability in Lima, where the government faces a vote of no confidence in parliament. Analysts said the strength of the data suggested that mine-level operations had become somewhat insulated from national political turbulence, at least in the near term.

"Peru's performance this month is an important reminder that the supply picture is not uniformly negative," said Julian Kettle, senior vice president for metals at Wood Mackenzie. "But even a 9% rise from Peru cannot fully offset what is being lost in Chile, and the structural demand growth from AI and electrification means the market will remain in deficit through this cycle."

Peru is the world's second-largest copper producer. Its record May output was nonetheless insufficient to prevent LME warehouse stocks from continuing their decline, which analysts attributed to equally strong demand from China, the United States and Europe.

The S&P Global Global Copper Supply-Demand Tracker for May revised the full-year 2026 supply deficit to 650,000 tonnes, up from the 500,000-tonne estimate published in April, reflecting both Chilean underperformance and an upward revision to demand from the technology and electrification sectors.

Tags: Peru, copper, Las Bambas, Quellaveco, Antamina, mining, supply, deficit, S&P Global

Sentiment signal: neutral to bullish
